questions.question:
The number of 3-digit numbers such that the digit 1 is never to the immediate right of 2 is
A. 781
B. 791
C. 881
D. 891
questions.to_find: count of 3-digit numbers where digit 1 does not immediately follow digit 2

understand.plain: We need to count all 3-digit numbers (100 to 999) that do NOT contain the substring '21' — meaning wherever 2 appears, the very next digit must not be 1. Total 3-digit numbers = 900. We subtract those that contain '21' as consecutive digits in positions (1,2) or (2,3).

keywords.explain: digit at position i+1 follows digit at position i; '1 is immediately to the right of 2' means the two-digit pattern 21 appears consecutively
keywords.example: In 213, digit 1 is immediately to the right of digit 2 (positions 1 and 2)

keywords.explain: count the forbidden cases and subtract from the total; easier when the forbidden set has a clean structure
keywords.example: total 3-digit numbers = 900; subtract those containing '21' as adjacent digits

keywords.explain: when forbidden patterns can overlap in multiple positions, add cases individually then subtract the overlap
keywords.example: numbers with '21' in positions (1,2) AND '21' in positions (2,3) would be 2-1-1 = 211; count it once by inclusion-exclusion

understand.visual_alt: 3-digit number with positions labeled hundreds, tens, units showing where pattern 21 can appear

given.aim: find count of 3-digit numbers with no '21' consecutive pair

terms.meaning: hundreds digit = 2, tens digit = 1; units digit can be 0-9
tens digit = 2, units digit = 1; hundreds digit can be 1-9

given.plan: Use complementary counting: forbidden = |A union B| = |A| + |B| - |A intersect B|. Then answer = 900 - |A union B|.

steps.title: Total 3-digit numbers
steps.apply: 3-digit numbers range from 100 to 999, giving 900 total numbers
steps.note: This is our starting count before any restriction

steps.title: Count |A|: '21' in positions (1,2)
steps.apply: Hundreds digit = 2 (fixed), tens digit = 1 (fixed), units digit = 0-9 (10 choices). |A| = 10.

steps.title: Count |B|: '21' in positions (2,3)
steps.apply: Hundreds digit = 1-9 (9 choices, cannot be 0), tens digit = 2 (fixed), units digit = 1 (fixed). |B| = 9.

steps.title: Count |A intersect B|: '21' in both positions
steps.apply: For '21' in positions (1,2) AND (2,3): hundreds=2, tens=1. But tens must also be 2 for the second pattern — contradiction (tens can't be both 1 and 2). Actually: positions (1,2)='21' means d1=2, d2=1; positions (2,3)='21' means d2=2, d3=1. These require d2=1 and d2=2 simultaneously — impossible. So |A intersect B| = 0.
steps.note: No 3-digit number can have '21' in BOTH adjacent position pairs simultaneously

questions.formula_note: Complementary counting combined with inclusion-exclusion on two forbidden pattern positions gives 900 - 19 = 881.

questions.question:
There are 4 women W_1, W_2, W_3, W_4 and 5 men M_1, M_2, M_3, M_4, M_5 in a group. We are required to form 4 pairs each consisting of one woman and one man. W_1 is not to be paired with M_1, and M_4 must necessarily be paired with someone. In how many ways can such 4 pairs be formed?
A. 74
B. 76
C. 78
D. 80
questions.to_find: number of ways to form 4 woman-man pairs given the two constraints

understand.plain: We need to pair 4 women with 4 of the 5 men (one man is excluded) such that W_1 is not paired with M_1 and M_4 is always included (never the excluded man). We use complementary counting: total pairings with M_4 always included minus those where W_1 is also paired with M_1.

keywords.explain: each woman is matched to exactly one man and vice versa; this is essentially a bijection from the 4 women to a chosen subset of 4 men
keywords.example: W_1-M_2, W_2-M_3, W_3-M_4, W_4-M_5 is one valid pairing

keywords.explain: M_4 cannot be the excluded man, so we always pick M_5 or one of M_1, M_2, M_3 to exclude — but M_4 stays
keywords.example: Exactly one of {M_1, M_2, M_3, M_5} is excluded; M_4 is always among the 4 men used

keywords.explain: any valid pairing must not assign M_1 to W_1
keywords.example: If M_1 is in the selected set, W_1 must be matched to one of the other 3 men

understand.visual_alt: Bipartite graph with 4 women on left, 5 men on right, showing M_4 always selected and W_1 not connected to M_1

given.aim: count valid pairings of 4 women with 4 of 5 men, M_4 always in, W_1 not with M_1

terms.meaning: exactly one man from {M_1, M_2, M_3, M_5} is left out (M_4 is never excluded)
terms.example: Case 1: M_1 excluded; Case 2: M_2 excluded; Case 3: M_3 excluded; Case 4: M_5 excluded
terms.connects: splits the problem into 4 cases based on which man is excluded

terms.meaning: number of ways to match 4 women to 4 chosen men with no restriction (a permutation)
terms.example: If men are {M_2,M_3,M_4,M_5}, there are 4! = 24 ways to assign them to W_1,W_2,W_3,W_4
terms.connects: base count for each case before applying the W_1-M_1 restriction

terms.meaning: when M_1 is in the selected set, we subtract pairings where W_1 is assigned M_1; those are 3! = 6
terms.example: Fix W_1-M_1; then pair remaining 3 women to 3 men in 3! = 6 ways
terms.connects: reduction applied only in cases where M_1 is present

given.plan: Case analysis by which man is excluded. Cases where M_1 is excluded: 24 valid pairings (no restriction applies). Cases where M_1 is included (3 sub-cases): 24 - 6 = 18 each. Sum all cases.

steps.title: Case A: M_1 is the excluded man
steps.apply: Men available: {M_2, M_3, M_4, M_5}. Since M_1 is absent, the W_1-M_1 restriction is automatically satisfied. All 4! = 24 pairings are valid.
steps.note: M_4 is included (constraint satisfied); W_1 cannot be with M_1 (M_1 absent).

steps.title: Case B: M_2 is excluded (M_1 is present)
steps.apply: Men: {M_1, M_3, M_4, M_5}. Total pairings = 24. Subtract forbidden: W_1 paired with M_1 and remaining 3 women matched to 3 men = 3! = 6. Valid = 18.
steps.note: The W_1-M_1 restriction reduces count by 6.

steps.title: Case C: M_3 is excluded (M_1 is present)
steps.apply: Men: {M_1, M_2, M_4, M_5}. By the same logic as Case B, valid pairings = 18.

steps.title: Case D: M_5 is excluded (M_1 is present)
steps.apply: Men: {M_1, M_2, M_3, M_4}. Valid pairings = 18.
steps.note: M_4 included; W_1 cannot be with M_1, so subtract 6.

questions.formula_note: Case-splitting by which man is excluded, then applying the forbidden-pair reduction (subtract 3! when M_1 is included) gives 24 + 3*18 = 78.

questions.question:
A three-member committee has to be formed from a group of 9 people. How many such distinct committees can be formed?
A. 27
B. 72
C. 81
D. 84
questions.to_find: number of distinct 3-member committees from a group of 9 people

understand.plain: A committee is an unordered selection of people — we care about who is on it, not the order they were chosen. We use combinations C(9,3) to count the number of ways to choose 3 people from 9 without regard to order. This is a direct application of the combination formula.

keywords.explain: number of ways to choose r items from n distinct items when order does not matter
keywords.example: C(5,2) = 10 ways to choose 2 people from 5

keywords.explain: a committee has no internal ordering — {Alice, Bob, Carol} and {Carol, Bob, Alice} are the same committee
keywords.example: choosing members for a committee vs arranging them in roles (which would be a permutation)

understand.visual_alt: 9 circles representing people with 3 highlighted as one committee selection

given.aim: count unordered 3-element subsets of a 9-person group

terms.meaning: total number of people available to choose from
terms.connects: top value in the combination formula

terms.meaning: number of people required on the committee
terms.example: committees like {P_1, P_2, P_3} or {P_4, P_7, P_9}
terms.connects: selection size in the combination formula

terms.meaning: 9! / (3! * 6!) — the exact count of distinct committees

steps.title: Identify the combination formula
steps.apply: We choose 3 people from 9, order irrelevant (committee membership, not positions). Use C(9,3).
steps.note: Order does not matter for committee membership.

steps.title: Compute C(9,3)
steps.note: Cancel 6! from numerator and denominator, leaving only the top 3 factors.

questions.formula_note: A committee is an unordered selection, so C(9,3) = 84 gives the distinct committee count directly.

questions.question:
Five different books (P, Q, R, S, T) are to be arranged on a shelf. The books R and S are to be arranged first and second, respectively from the right side of the shelf. The number of different orders in which P, Q and T may be arranged is ______.
A. 2
B. 6
C. 12
D. 120
questions.to_find: number of arrangements of books P, Q, T in the three remaining positions on the shelf

understand.plain: The shelf has 5 positions. R occupies position 5 (rightmost) and S occupies position 4 (second from right). These two books are fixed. The remaining 3 books — P, Q, T — can fill the 3 remaining positions (positions 1, 2, 3 from the left) in any order. The number of ways to arrange 3 distinct books in 3 positions is 3! = 6.

keywords.explain: R and S have assigned spots (positions 4 and 5 from the left), leaving 3 free slots for P, Q, T
keywords.example: Shelf: [_ _ _ S R] — only the first three positions are free

keywords.explain: 3 distinct books in 3 ordered positions give 3! = 6 distinct arrangements
keywords.example: PQT, PTQ, QPT, QTP, TPQ, TQP are the 6 arrangements of P, Q, T

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='380' height='110' viewBox='0 0 380 110'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='20' width='60' height='60' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='40' y='57' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>?</text><rect x='80' y='20' width='60' height='60' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='110' y='57' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>?</text><rect x='150' y='20' width='60' height='60' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='180' y='57' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>?</text><rect x='220' y='20' width='60' height='60' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='250' y='57' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>S</text><rect x='290' y='20' width='60' height='60' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='320' y='57' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>R</text><text x='40' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>P1</text><text x='110' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>P2</text><text x='180' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>P3</text><text x='250' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>P4</text><text x='320' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>P5</text><text x='5' y='100' text-anchor='start' class='sub mut'>L</text><text x='355' y='100' text-anchor='start' class='sub mut'>R</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: 5 shelf slots with S fixed in position 4 and R fixed in position 5 (from left), leaving positions 1-3 free for P, Q, T

given.aim: count arrangements of P, Q, T in the 3 free positions after fixing R and S

terms.meaning: first from right means the last position on the shelf
terms.connects: fixes position 5, reducing free slots to 4

terms.meaning: second from right is the fourth position
terms.connects: fixes position 4, reducing free slots to 3

terms.meaning: 3 distinct books, 3 ordered positions — a full permutation of 3 items
terms.example: P at pos 1, Q at pos 2, T at pos 3 is one arrangement

given.plan: Fix R and S. Count permutations of P, Q, T in the remaining 3 ordered positions using P(3,3) = 3! = 6.

steps.title: Fix positions of R and S
steps.formula_raw: R at position 5, S at position 4 (both fixed)
steps.apply: 'First from right' = position 5; 'second from right' = position 4. Assign R to pos 5 and S to pos 4. These 2 books are now fixed.
steps.note: No choice is made here; R and S have exactly one valid placement each.

steps.title: Arrange P, Q, T in remaining 3 positions
steps.apply: Positions 1, 2, 3 are free. Arrange 3 distinct books P, Q, T in 3 ordered slots: 3 * 2 * 1 = 6.
steps.note: The 6 arrangements are: PQT, PTQ, QPT, QTP, TPQ, TQP.

questions.formula_note: After fixing R and S in their mandated positions, the remaining 3 books P, Q, T fill 3 ordered slots in 3! = 6 ways.

questions.question:
Person 1 and Person 2 invest in three mutual funds A, B and C. The amounts they invest in each of these mutual funds are given in the table.

| | Mutual fund A | Mutual fund B | Mutual fund C |
|---|---|---|---|
| Person 1 | 10,000 | 20,000 | 15,000 |
| Person 2 | 20,000 | 10,000 | 15,000 |

At the end of one year, the total amount that Person 1 gets is Rs. 500 more than Person 2. The annual rate of return for the mutual funds B and C is 15% each. What is the annual rate of return for mutual fund A?
A. 7.5%
B. 10%
C. 15%
D. 20%
questions.to_find: annual rate of return for mutual fund A

understand.plain: Person 1 and Person 2 invest different amounts in the same three mutual funds. After one year, the return from each fund equals (amount invested * annual rate). Person 1's total return exceeds Person 2's total return by Rs. 500. Since B and C both earn 15%, we can compute and cancel those terms, leaving a single linear equation in the unknown rate r_A.

keywords.explain: return for one year = principal * rate; no compounding involved in a one-year simple return problem
keywords.example: 10,000 invested at 10% earns 1,000 at the end of one year

keywords.explain: instead of computing absolute totals, compute Return_1 - Return_2 = 500 and solve for the unknown rate
keywords.example: funds B and C have identical rates so their per-person difference cancels; only fund A creates a net difference

keywords.explain: after subtracting Person 2's return from Person 1's return, the result is a simple linear equation: coefficient * r_A = constant

understand.visual_alt: Table with Person 1 and Person 2 rows and Mutual Funds A, B, C as columns showing investment amounts

given.aim: find rate r_A such that Person 1's total one-year return exceeds Person 2's by Rs. 500

terms.connects: forms the left-hand side of the return equation
forms the right-hand side of the return equation

terms.meaning: fund A has unknown annual rate; B and C each earn 15% per year
terms.example: Fund C: Person 1 earns 15000*0.15 = 2250; Person 2 earns 15000*0.15 = 2250 — their difference = 0
terms.connects: fund C contributions cancel; fund B difference = (20000 - 10000)*0.15 = 1500 in favour of Person 1

given.plan: Compute Return_1 - Return_2 = 500, expand, simplify. Fund C cancels. Isolate and solve for r_A.

steps.title: Write Person 1's total one-year return
steps.note: Fund B earns 3000 and Fund C earns 2250 for Person 1.

steps.title: Write Person 2's total one-year return
steps.note: Person 2 invests more in A (20k vs 10k) but less in B (10k vs 20k); C is the same for both.

steps.title: Set up equation: Return_1 - Return_2 = 500
steps.note: Note how fund C (15,000 each at 15%) contributes equally and cancels; fund B creates the 1500 advantage for Person 1.

steps.title: Solve for r_A
steps.note: The negative sign means Person 2 invests more in A; still Person 1 ends up ahead due to the larger B investment advantage.

questions.formula_note: Simple annual return is principal times rate; subtracting Person 2's return from Person 1's return and setting it equal to 500 gives the linear equation -10000*r_A + 1500 = 500, yielding r_A = 10%.
